Article.ArticleTitle: Inductively Coupled Plasma Mass Spectrometry Analysis (ICP-MS) and Particle Size Distribution of Commercially Available Herbal and Nonherbal Toothpastes.

Abstract.AbstractText: The effectiveness and safety of toothpaste formulations, both herbal and nonherbal, are influenced by a number of ingredients, such as detergents, sweeteners, and abrasives.

Using Inductively Coupled Plasma mass spectrometry, this study examined the chemical makeup of two widely available toothpastes in India: Dabur Red, which is herbal, and Colgate Strong Teeth, nonherbal. The analysis concentrated on the amounts of sweeteners, abrasive agents, and other important components.

The findings showed that nonherbal toothpastes, especially Colgate Strong Care, had higher concentrations of sodium saccharine, sorbitol, hydrated silica, and calcium carbonate, all of which increased abrasivity. Lower amounts of these compounds were found in herbal toothpastes, indicating a softer formulation. The anticariogenic and antihypersensitive qualities of potassium nitrate and fluoride were unique to Colgate.
This study emphasizes the unique qualities of herbal and nonherbal toothpaste formulations, highlighting the necessity of striking a balance between cleansing effectiveness and safety.
Abstract.CopyrightInformation: Copyright: © 2025 Journal of Pharmacy and Bioallied Sciences.

AffiliationInfo.Affiliation: Department of Dental Science, Bharath Institute of Higher Education and Research, Chennai, Tamil Nadu, India.

Department of Pharmacology, Sree Balaji Dental College and Hospital, Bharath Institute of Higher Education and Research, Chennai, Tamil Nadu, India.

Associate Professor, Chief Scientist, Department of Conservative Dentistry and Endodontics, White Lab-Material Research Centre, Saveetha Dental College and Hospital, Saveetha Institute of Medical and Technical Sciences, Saveetha University, Chennai, Tamil Nadu, India.

MedlineCitation.CoiStatement: There are no conflicts of interest.
